task: I’m putting together a short post-production checklist for a freelance editor before picture lock, and I want it to catch the stuff that usually slips through. Can you help me draft one that’s practical and not overkill?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a practical **pre-picture-lock post-production checklist** for a freelance editor that focuses on the common things that slip through without getting too heavy.

---

# Pre–Picture Lock Checklist for Editor

## 1) Editorial continuity
- [ ] Watch the cut start to finish for story clarity and pacing
- [ ] Check scene/shot continuity for obvious jumps, missing reactions, or mismatched actions
- [ ] Confirm all intended story beats are present
- [ ] Verify no temporary placeholders are still in the cut
- [ ] Make sure all alt takes, inserts, and pickups have been considered

## 2) Picture and timeline hygiene
- [ ] Remove any unused clips, stray audio, or hidden layers
- [ ] Check for accidental overlaps, gaps, or black frames
- [ ] Confirm no offline media, red renders, or missing files
- [ ] Verify all freeze frames, speed ramps, and retimes are intentional
- [ ] Check that all titles, graphics, and supers are correct and spelled properly

## 3) Audio sanity check
- [ ] Listen for pops, clicks, hums, clipped audio, or abrupt edits
- [ ] Make sure temp music is balanced and not masking dialogue
- [ ] Confirm dialogue is intelligible throughout
- [ ] Check for missing SFX, room tone, or wild lines that should be in place
- [ ] Verify any audio fades are smooth and intentional

## 4) Text, names, and legal details
- [ ] Spell-check all on-screen text, names, locations, and credits
- [ ] Confirm lower-thirds and end credits are accurate
- [ ] Check brand names, dates, and factual text for correctness
- [ ] Make sure any required legal text/disclaimers are included

## 5) Versioning and exports
- [ ] Confirm project name, cut version, and date are clearly labeled
- [ ] Export a review file and verify it plays correctly
- [ ] Check that timecode, burn-ins, and frame rate are correct if needed
- [ ] Save a clean backup of the project before lock
- [ ] Note any unresolved issues for the producer/director

## 6) Final review pass
- [ ] Watch the cut once without stopping
- [ ] Watch the cut once with notes or on mute if needed for visual issues
- [ ] Confirm there are no last-minute “obvious fixes” left
- [ ] Get sign-off from the decision-maker before locking

---

## Optional “quick catch” items
These are the ones that often get missed:
- [ ] One last check for spelling in supers and credits
- [ ] No temp music cues left in the cut
- [ ] No missing frames at cuts or dissolves
- [ ] No accidental muted tracks or soloed tracks
- [ ] No mismatched aspect ratio or scaling issues
- [ ] No duplicate shots or accidental repeated sections

---

If you want, I can also turn this into:
1. a **one-page client-friendly checklist**,  
2. a **more technical editor version**, or  
3. a **checkbox table you can paste into Notion/Google Docs**.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty close. But I’d tighten it a bit and make the “slips through” stuff more specific to actual edit-room pain.

What I’d want added:
- **sync check** — especially if it’s multicam or mixed sources
- **handles / pre-lay / temp VFX** status if anything’s still living in the cut
- **sequence settings** sanity check: frame rate, aspect ratio, field/order if relevant
- **audio channel mapping** if there’s any deliverable risk there
- **version control / archive note** — what’s locked, what’s still open, where the clean project lives

Also, the “watch it once with notes or on mute” line feels a little generic. Better to say something like:
- watch once for story
- watch once for technical issues

If you want, I can turn this into a leaner one-pager that feels more like something a working freelance editor would actually use, not a template trying too hard to be complete.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, more edit-room practical version that bakes in the stuff that actually gets missed.

---

# Picture Lock Checklist for Freelance Editor

## 1) Story and cut integrity
- [ ] Watch the cut start to finish for story clarity, pacing, and performance
- [ ] Confirm all intended scenes, beats, and reactions are present
- [ ] Check for accidental trims, repeated sections, or missing moments
- [ ] Make sure any temp placeholders are intentional and clearly flagged

## 2) Sync and sequence sanity
- [ ] Check sync on all critical dialogue, especially multicam or mixed-source material
- [ ] Verify no drift, slipped clips, or out-of-sync inserts
- [ ] Confirm sequence settings are correct:
  - [ ] frame rate
  - [ ] aspect ratio / resolution
  - [ ] field order or interlace settings if relevant
- [ ] Check for unintended speed changes, retimes, freezes, or reversed shots

## 3) Picture / timeline hygiene
- [ ] Remove or flag any unused clips, stray layers, or hidden temp elements
- [ ] Check for black frames, gaps, bad edits, or accidental overlaps
- [ ] Confirm no offline media, missing renders, or red/missing items
- [ ] Verify any temp VFX, pre-lays, or placeholders are labeled and tracked
- [ ] Confirm handles are still available if anything needs conforming or finishing

## 4) Audio checks
- [ ] Listen for pops, clicks, hum, clipping, or abrupt audio cuts
- [ ] Check dialogue intelligibility throughout
- [ ] Confirm temp music and SFX are not masking key dialogue
- [ ] Verify audio fades, room tone, wild lines, and pre-lays are clean
- [ ] Check audio channel mapping / track routing if deliverables depend on it

## 5) Text, graphics, and credits
- [ ] Spell-check all supers, lower-thirds, names, locations, and credits
- [ ] Confirm any on-screen dates, brand names, and factual text are correct
- [ ] Make sure any required legal text or disclaimers are included
- [ ] Check that graphics are in the right place, size, and safe area

## 6) Version control and archive
- [ ] Confirm the locked version name/date is clear and consistent
- [ ] Save a clean archive copy of the project and key assets
- [ ] Note what is locked vs. what remains open for post
- [ ] Store the clean project in the agreed location
- [ ] Flag any unresolved issues for producer/director before lock

## 7) Final passes
- [ ] Watch once for story
- [ ] Watch once for technical issues
- [ ] Confirm there are no obvious fixes left before lock
- [ ] Get final sign-off before freezing the cut

---

## Common “slips through” items
- [ ] Sync drift in multicam or mixed-source sequences
- [ ] Wrong frame rate / aspect ratio / field order settings
- [ ] Temp VFX, pre-lays, or placeholders left unlabeled
- [ ] Audio channel mapping not matching delivery needs
- [ ] Handles missing on anything that may need online/conform
- [ ] Clean project/archive not saved or not clearly versioned

If you want, I can also turn this into:
- a **super-short one-page checklist**, or
- a **copy/paste Notion/Google Docs version** with checkboxes.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is closer. Still a little clean-clean for what actually bites you in the room, though.

I’d want two small tweaks:
- make **sync** and **sequence settings** part of the main checklist, not buried in “common slips through”
- add one line for **online/conform risk** — basically, if anything is going to need reconform, VFX turnover, or delivery prep later, note it now

And the archive bit should be a little more explicit. Something like:
- what’s locked
- what’s still open
- where the clean project / archive lives
- who has the last known good version

That’s the kind of thing people forget when they’re half a day from lock and everybody’s just trying to get out.

If you want, I can tighten this one more step into a true working-editor version — shorter, less polished, more like a checklist someone would actually keep open on the second monitor.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a more working-editor version: tighter, practical, and a little less polished.

# Pre-Lock Checklist for Freelance Editor

## 1) Story / cut pass
- [ ] Watch the cut start to finish for story, pacing, and performance
- [ ] Confirm all intended scenes, beats, and reactions are in
- [ ] Check for missing moments, accidental trims, or repeated sections
- [ ] Flag any temp placeholders still in the cut

## 2) Sync / sequence settings
- [ ] Check sync on dialogue, multicam, and mixed-source material
- [ ] Look for drift, slipped clips, or out-of-sync inserts
- [ ] Confirm sequence settings are correct:
  - [ ] frame rate
  - [ ] aspect ratio / resolution
  - [ ] field order / interlace if relevant
- [ ] Check for unintended retimes, freezes, speed changes, or reversed shots

## 3) Picture / timeline hygiene
- [ ] Remove or flag unused clips, stray layers, and hidden temp elements
- [ ] Check for black frames, gaps, bad edits, or accidental overlaps
- [ ] Confirm no offline media, missing renders, or red/missing items
- [ ] Make sure temp VFX, pre-lays, and placeholders are labeled
- [ ] Note any handles needed for conform, finishing, or VFX

## 4) Audio
- [ ] Listen for pops, clicks, hum, clipping, or abrupt cuts
- [ ] Check dialogue intelligibility throughout
- [ ] Make sure temp music and SFX aren’t masking key dialogue
- [ ] Verify fades, room tone, wild lines, and pre-lays are clean
- [ ] Check audio channel mapping / track routing if delivery depends on it

## 5) Text / graphics / credits
- [ ] Spell-check supers, lower-thirds, names, locations, and credits
- [ ] Confirm on-screen dates, brand names, and factual text
- [ ] Make sure required legal text / disclaimers are included
- [ ] Check graphics placement, size, and safe area

## 6) Online / conform / delivery risk
- [ ] Note anything that will need reconform, VFX turnover, or finishing
- [ ] Flag any shots that may need clean plates, handles, or versioning later
- [ ] Confirm anything temporary is clearly marked for online/post
- [ ] List any open delivery concerns before lock

## 7) Version control / archive
- [ ] Confirm what is locked and what is still open
- [ ] Save the clean project / archive in the agreed location
- [ ] Make sure the last known good version is clearly identified
- [ ] Note who has the last known good version
- [ ] Keep a simple version/date naming convention

## 8) Final pass
- [ ] Watch once for story
- [ ] Watch once for technical issues
- [ ] Fix or flag anything obvious before lock
- [ ] Get final sign-off before freezing the cut

If you want, I can also make this:
- even **shorter and more blunt**, or
- formatted as a **checkbox list for Notion / Google Docs**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah — that’s basically the right shape. Short enough to use, but it actually catches the stuff that burns you later. The sync / sequence settings / online-conform / archive split is cleaner now, and the final passes line is better that way.

I’d use this.
